ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for salt-and-pepper deep-fried duck rack/carcass. FOODON_03311737 (duck food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers duck meat broadly; no specific code for salt-and-pepper fried duck rack preparation exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for salt-and-pepper duck rack as a prepared dish.

preparation_method: seasoned with salt and white pepper

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of salt and pepper duck rack is approximately 350 g, representing one whole duck carcass/frame portion as served at a Chinese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Duck, domesticated, meat and skin, cooked, roasted — adapted with deep-frying adjustment for salt-and-pepper preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central — Foundation Foods / SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for roasted duck meat and skin (NDB 05141) used as the closest defensible base. Values adjusted upward for fat and energy to reflect deep-frying in oil. Sodium elevated to reflect salt seasoning. The duck rack is predominantly bone with attached meat and skin; edible yield estimated at approximately 55–60% of total weight. Nutrient values are expressed per 100 g of the as-served edible portion (meat + skin + absorbed fry oil). Carbohydrate contribution from batter/coating is minimal as traditional salt-and-pepper duck rack uses no thick batter; a small starch contribution from light dusting is included.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein- and fat-dominant meat dishes — analogous estimate based on roasted/fried duck and poultry entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salt-and-pepper duck rack. GI estimated at 15 based on the principle that high-protein, high-fat meat dishes with minimal carbohydrate content have very low glycemic impact. Analogous poultry and meat entries in the University of Sydney GI Database consistently show GI values in the 0–20 range. Carbohydrate per 100 g is 2.5 g; GL per 350 g serving = (15 × 2.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 1.3, rounded to 0.9 per 100 g equivalent. GL per serving (350 g) = approximately 1.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from salt seasoning and deep-frying process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid salt-and-pepper duck rack due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,820 mg per restaurant serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Salt-and-pepper duck rack has a very low carbohydrate content (approximately 2.5 g per 100 g) and a low estimated glycemic index of 15, resulting in minimal postprandial glucose impact; however, the high fat content and caloric density warrant portion control for individuals managing weight alongside Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium from deep-frying and seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of approximately 6.2 g saturated fat, 95 mg cholesterol, and 520 mg sodium per 100 g makes salt-and-pepper duck rack a food that individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume only occasionally and in small portions, consistent with heart-healthy dietary guidelines recommending reduced saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salt and Pepper Duck Rack (椒盐鸭架, jiāo yán yā jià) is a popular Chinese dish in which the bony carcass or frame of a roasted or fresh duck is deep-fried until golden and crispy, then tossed in a wok with salt, white pepper, garlic, chilli, and spring onion. The dish is a staple of Cantonese and northern Chinese cuisine and is widely served in Chinese restaurants across Singapore, where it is valued as a flavourful, economical use of the duck carcass after the breast and leg meat have been removed. The texture is characteristically crispy on the outside with small pockets of tender, flavourful meat clinging to the bones, and the aroma is dominated by toasted white pepper and garlic. Nutritionally, the edible portion is high in protein (approximately 22.5 g per 100 g) and fat (approximately 21 g per 100 g), with a moderate caloric density of approximately 290 kcal per 100 g. The carbohydrate content is very low (approximately 2.5 g per 100 g), giving the dish a low glycemic index estimated at 15. The sodium content is elevated at approximately 520 mg per 100 g due to the salt seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans and is not certified halal due to the use of duck. It is gluten-free in its traditional preparation. A typical restaurant serving is approximately 350 g, providing roughly 1,015 kcal, 78.75 g protein, 73.5 g fat, and approximately 1,820 mg sodium.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for salt-and-pepper duck rack as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy roasted duck (meat and skin) with adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption and salt seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database principles for high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA duck data is a strong compositional anchor and the adaptations are methodologically defensible.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted USDA SR Legacy source entry for duck meat and skin. All other micronutrients with -1 values have been replaced with estimated values where defensible USDA data exists. The duck rack is a bony cut; the edible yield is approximately 55–60% of total served weight. Nutrient values are expressed per 100 g of edible portion. Sodium value of 520 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant-level salt seasoning; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. The dish is not certified halal as duck slaughter certification varies by restaurant. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 350 g serving is approximately 1.3 (effectively negligible). The glycemic load per serving field in the record reflects the per-100 g GL equivalent of 0.9.
